ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani food product class; no specific prawn/jheenga dum biryani subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers biryani as a category. No specific prawn dum biryani code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for jheenga dum biryani or prawn biryani as a specific food concept.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of jheenga dum biryani is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous portion of basmati rice layered with tiger prawns and aromatic spices.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn Biryani / Shrimp Biryani — composite estimate based on ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) entries for cooked basmati rice, cooked prawns, and biryani-style preparations; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked shrimp and cooked white rice
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry for jheenga dum biryani as a composite dish. Values estimated from ICMR-NIN data for cooked basmati rice (~130 kcal/100g, 2.7g protein, 0.4g fat, 28g carb) and cooked prawns (~105 kcal/100g, 20g protein, 1.7g fat, 0.9g carb), combined in approximate ratio of 60% rice to 40% prawn with ghee and spice contribution, yielding a blended per-100g profile consistent with published Indian seafood biryani analyses. USDA FoodData Central NDB 15271 (shrimp, cooked) and NDB 20444 (rice, white, long-grain, cooked) used for cross-validation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of basmati rice and mixed rice-based dishes — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice (GI ~50-58) and mixed rice dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for jheenga dum biryani. GI estimated at 50 based on basmati rice as the dominant carbohydrate source (GI 50–58 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008). The dum cooking method, presence of protein (prawns), fat (ghee), and fibre (spices, onion) further attenuate glycemic response, supporting a conservative estimate at the lower end of the basmati range. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 70) / 100 = 35.0, based on 400g serving with 17.5g carbohydrate per 100g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg/100g; ~1520 mg per 400g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250g and request reduced salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 1520 mg sodium, representing over 65% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~35 per 400g serving) from basmati rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200g) to limit glycemic load, noting that basmati rice has a lower GI (~50) than regular white rice, and the protein and fat content from prawns and ghee further moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol from prawns (~95 mg/100g) and saturated fat from ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate intake due to the cholesterol contribution from tiger prawns and saturated fat from ghee; however, prawns are low in total fat and rich in omega-3 fatty acids, and occasional moderate portions are generally acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jheenga Dum Biryani is a classic North Indian seafood rice dish in which whole tiger prawns (jheenga) are slow-cooked together with long-grain basmati rice using the traditional dum method — a sealed-pot technique that traps steam and allows the rice and prawns to cook simultaneously in their own aromatic juices. The dish is built by first marinating the prawns in yoghurt, ginger-garlic paste, and spices, then layering them with parboiled basmati rice, fried onions, saffron-infused milk, ghee, fresh mint, and coriander before sealing the pot with dough or a tight lid and cooking over low heat. The result is a fragrant, moist, and richly spiced rice dish in which each grain of basmati remains separate and perfumed with cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and saffron. The prawns are tender and juicy, absorbing the spice marinade while contributing a naturally sweet seafood flavour. Nutritionally, jheenga dum biryani provides a moderate energy density (~148 kcal/100g), a meaningful protein contribution from tiger prawns (~9.8g/100g), and a moderate carbohydrate load from basmati rice (~17.5g/100g) with a relatively low glycemic index (~50) compared to regular white rice. The dish contains cholesterol from prawns and saturated fat from ghee, making portion control relevant for individuals managing cardiovascular risk or Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is elevated due to salt and spice blends used in restaurant preparation. In Singapore, this dish is served at North Indian restaurants and is popular as a celebratory or weekend meal. It is typically halal-certified when served at Indian Muslim establishments.
common_user_queries: How many calories in prawn biryani?
Is prawn biryani good for diabetics?
What is the GI of biryani?
What spices are in dum biryani?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for jheenga dum biryani as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients (cooked basmati rice and cooked tiger prawns) combined in a representative ratio, with ghee and spice contributions included. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and cross-validated but not based on a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN (2017) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked basmati rice and cooked tiger prawns in an approximate 60:40 ratio with ghee contribution; no direct approved-source entry for jheenga dum biryani exists. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this estimation. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries used for this estimation. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in the component ingredients per USDA SR Legacy data. 5. GI estimated at 50 based on basmati rice (University of Sydney GI Database); dum cooking method and protein/fat content from prawns and ghee support a conservative lower-end estimate. 6. Glycemic load per 400g serving = (50 × 17.5 × 4) / 100 = 35.0. 7. The food_name canonical form uses 'Biryani' (standard spelling); the input used 'Briyani' (common Singapore variant), which is preserved in aliases. 8. Halal status is likely for restaurant preparations at Indian Muslim establishments but cannot be universally confirmed for all serving restaurants; flagged as true based on typical North Indian Muslim restaurant practice. 9. Sodium estimate (~380 mg/100g) reflects typical restaurant-level salting; home preparations may be lower.
